blocks.text: Thinking, Fast and Slow, by Daniel Kahneman. The book map.

Kahneman's core claim is that your mind runs on two systems. System 1 is fast, automatic, associative, and effortless — it reads faces, completes patterns, and generates impressions you experience as truth. System 2 is slow, deliberate, and lazy — it can check System 1's work but usually just endorses it. Most cognitive bias is System 1 answering a hard question by silently substituting an easier one, and System 2 failing to notice.

The book builds this argument in five movements. Part I describes the two systems and the associative machinery — priming, cognitive ease, the rule Kahneman calls WYSIATI (what you see is all there is). Part II catalogs the heuristics that machinery produces: anchoring, availability, representativeness, and our blindness to statistics and regression to the mean. Part III shows how these errors compound into overconfidence — the illusions of understanding and validity, the planning fallacy, and misplaced faith in expert intuition. Part IV presents prospect theory, the Nobel-winning account of how we actually choose under risk: we respond to gains and losses, not wealth, and losses hurt roughly twice as much. Part V closes with the two selves — the experiencing self that lives your life and the remembering self that scores it, badly.
The payoff is not self-cure (Kahneman doubts that) but a vocabulary for spotting these errors in others, in institutions, and occasionally in yourself.
How to choose

Chapters 1 and 3 are the essential core — every later idea depends on the two systems and WYSIATI. Chapters 4-6 matter most if you forecast, hire, or plan projects. Chapters 7-8 are for anyone who negotiates, invests, or designs choices. Chapter 9 stands alone; read it if you think about happiness or how to design experiences.
Two Systems, One Lazy Controller

The mind runs two fictional characters. System 1 operates automatically and effortlessly — it detects hostility in a voice, completes '2+2', and drives on an empty road. System 2 allocates attention to effortful work — filling a tax form, computing 17×24 — and experiences itself as the author of your choices, though it mostly rubber-stamps System 1's suggestions. Effort is physically real: in Kahneman's Add-1 task, pupils dilate with cognitive load, and Chabris and Simons' invisible-gorilla study shows attention is a scarce budget — absorbed viewers literally don't see a gorilla. System 2 is also lazy. The bat-and-ball problem (they cost $1.10, the bat costs $1 more; the ball is 5 cents, not 10) fools most students at elite universities because checking feels like work. Baumeister's ego depletion studies suggest self-control draws on a shared, exhaustible resource. Stanovich's distinction between intelligence and rationality explains why smart people still answer badly: engagement, not IQ, is what catches System 1's errors. Takeaway: you cannot turn System 1 off; you can only learn when to slow down.
The Associative Machine

Ideas activate each other in a spreading cascade you never experience directly. Priming shows the cascade steering behavior: in Bargh's Florida effect, students unscrambling words about old age walked away more slowly; Vohs found money-primed people became more selfish and self-reliant; the Lady Macbeth effect links moral disgust to a literal urge to wash. (Kahneman flags that these findings deserve skepticism — some later failed to replicate.) Cognitive ease is the master signal: anything easy to process feels true, familiar, and good. Zajonc's mere exposure effect shows repetition alone breeds liking; clear fonts and rhyming aphorisms are judged more credible — the illusion of truth. The Moses illusion ('How many animals did Moses take on the ark?') shows System 1 checking plausibility, not accuracy. The machine also insists on causality: in Michotte's experiments people see one shape 'launch' another, and Kahneman's norm theory explains how single surprises quietly rewrite what we expect next. Takeaway: your sense that something is true is often just a report that it was easy to think.
Jumping to Conclusions: WYSIATI and Substitution

System 1 builds the most coherent story it can from available evidence and never asks what's missing — Kahneman's acronym is WYSIATI: what you see is all there is. Coherence, not completeness, produces confidence. The halo effect makes one known trait color all the unknowns ('Alan: intelligent, stubborn' beats the same list reversed), and first impressions dominate. Todorov found that a one-tenth-second glance at candidates' faces — rating mere competence — predicted about 70% of Senate races: System 1's basic assessments leak into consequential votes. The deepest mechanism is substitution: faced with a hard question (Should I invest in Ford? How happy am I?), System 1 answers an easier one (Do I like Ford cars? What's my mood?) without noticing the swap. The affect heuristic (Slovic) is the master substitution — likes and dislikes determine beliefs, so people who love a technology rate its risks low and benefits high. In one study, students asked about dating before life satisfaction let the dating answer become the happiness answer. Takeaway: confidence measures the coherence of your story, not the quality of your evidence.
The Heuristics: Anchors, Availability, and Linda

Four workhorse biases. The law of small numbers: we trust tiny samples. The counties with the lowest kidney-cancer rates are small and rural — and so are those with the highest, because small samples produce extremes; the Gates Foundation funded small schools partly on this artifact. Anchoring: any number in view drags your estimate. A rigged wheel of fortune shifted estimates of African UN membership; experienced real-estate agents were swayed by listing prices while denying it; the measured anchoring index runs about 55%. Availability: we judge frequency by ease of recall, so tornadoes seem deadlier than asthma. Slovic's work and the availability cascade (the Alar apple scare) show media loops inflating minor risks into policy panics. Representativeness: we judge probability by similarity, ignoring base rates — Tom W. 'seems like' a computer-science student, so the tiny size of that field is ignored. Its sharpest form is the conjunction fallacy: most people rate 'Linda is a feminist bank teller' as more probable than 'Linda is a bank teller,' which is logically impossible. Hsee's dinnerware study adds less-is-more: adding broken pieces lowered the set's judged value. Takeaway: plausible stories beat probability every time — unless you force the statistics into view.
Regression to the Mean and Taming Predictions

Extreme performance is partly luck, so it is followed by something closer to average — regression to the mean. Israeli flight instructors concluded that praise hurts and screaming works, because a great landing was typically followed by a worse one and vice versa; they mistook regression for the effect of feedback. The 'Sports Illustrated jinx' and the sophomore slump are the same arithmetic: an athlete on a cover had an unusually lucky season. Kahneman's formulas — success = talent + luck; great success = a little more talent + a lot more luck — capture why regression is guaranteed whenever correlation between two measures is imperfect. Yet System 1 demands causal stories, so we invent explanations for what is mere statistical shrinkage. Intuitive predictions ignore this: asked to predict a student's GPA from a childhood reading score, people match the prediction's extremeness to the evidence. The fix is a four-step discipline: start from the baseline (the average outcome), estimate your intuitive prediction, judge the correlation between evidence and outcome, and move only that proportion of the distance from baseline toward intuition. Takeaway: whenever performance swings back toward normal, suspect math before causes.
Overconfidence: Illusions, Formulas, and the Premortem

Part III's theme: we vastly overrate how much we understand. The narrative fallacy (Taleb) makes the past seem inevitable — Google's history reads like destiny only in hindsight. Hindsight bias (Fischhoff): once you know an outcome you can't recover your prior uncertainty, so decision-makers get blamed for good bets that failed (outcome bias). The illusion of validity: Kahneman's army leadership assessments barely predicted officer performance, yet his confidence in each judgment never dimmed. Odean and Barber showed the same in finance — the most active individual traders performed worst, and stock-picking skill is largely an illusion of skill sustained by a professional culture. Meehl's classic finding: across ~200 tested domains, simple statistical formulas matched or beat clinical expert judgment — clear wins in roughly 60% of studies, statistical ties in the rest (and a tie favors the cheaper formula); the Apgar score saved newborns by replacing intuition with five rated dimensions. When can intuition be trusted? Kahneman's adversarial collaboration with Gary Klein concluded: only in regular environments with rapid, clear feedback (chess, firefighting — not stock markets or politics). The planning fallacy — his own textbook took 8+ years after a 2-year estimate — is cured by reference-class forecasting (Flyvbjerg): base estimates on similar past projects. Against organizational optimism, Klein's premortem: 'Assume the plan failed — write its history.'
Prospect Theory: Losses Loom Larger

Bernoulli's 300-year-old utility theory said people value states of wealth. Bernoulli's error: they actually value changes relative to a reference point — Jack and Jill can hold identical wealth today and feel opposite emotions depending on where they started. Prospect theory (Kahneman and Tversky's Nobel-winning model) rests on three features: reference dependence, diminishing sensitivity (the difference between $100 and $200 feels bigger than between $900 and $1,000), and loss aversion — losses hurt roughly twice as much as equivalent gains please, so most people refuse a coin flip unless they can win about $200 against a $100 loss. This yields the fourfold pattern: risk-averse for probable gains (take the sure thing), risk-seeking for probable losses (gamble to avoid a sure loss — why litigants fight doomed cases), risk-seeking for improbable gains (lotteries), risk-averse for improbable losses (insurance). Loss aversion also produces the endowment effect: in Thaler's mug experiments, owners demanded about twice what buyers would pay, purely because selling feels like a loss. Reference points include goals: cab drivers quit after hitting daily targets, and pro golfers putt measurably better for par than for birdie. Takeaway: every choice is framed against a reference point — find it and you can predict the decision.
Risky Choices: Rare Events, Mental Accounts, and Frames

Prospect theory applied. Rare events are overweighted when vivid (terrorism after bus bombings, lottery jackpots) and underweighted when learned from experience — and denominator neglect means 'kills 1,286 in 10,000' scares more than '12.86%.' The fix for case-by-case timidity is a risk policy: Samuelson's colleague refused one favorable bet but should accept a hundred — broad framing wins, so 'you win a few, you lose a few.' Thaler's mental accounting shows we keep separate emotional books: people sell winning stocks to feel gains and keep losers (the disposition effect), and honor sunk costs by escalating doomed projects. Anticipated regret distorts choices — deviations from default feel worse than inaction, which is why people demand absurd premiums to accept any risk they caused. Preference reversals: evaluated jointly, people pay more for the intact dinnerware set; evaluated alone, judgments flip — context determines value. Framing: '90% survival' beats '10% mortality' even among physicians; the Asian disease problem flips choices between identical options worded as lives saved versus lives lost; organ-donation rates track opt-in versus opt-out defaults, not national character. Takeaway: there is often no 'true' preference underneath — the frame is the choice.
The Two Selves: Memory Against Experience

You contain an experiencing self that lives each moment and a remembering self that keeps score — and the rememberer, who makes all your decisions, is a bad accountant. It obeys the peak-end rule (a memory's value is the average of the worst/best moment and the final moment) and duration neglect (length barely counts). In the cold-hand experiment, people preferred to repeat a longer painful trial because it ended slightly warmer; in Redelmeier's colonoscopy study, patients whose procedures ended gently remembered less pain regardless of duration. A vacation you'd skip if all photos and memories were erased reveals which self you're serving. Measuring well-being splits the same way: life-evaluation questions poll the remembering self, while the Day Reconstruction Method and U-index (share of time in unpleasant states) capture experience — and beyond roughly $75,000 (2011 dollars), income improves evaluation but not experienced feeling. The focusing illusion — "Nothing in life is as important as you think it is, while you are thinking about it" (Kahneman) — explains why Californians aren't happier and paraplegics aren't as miserable as we predict. The conclusion revisits Econs versus Humans and endorses libertarian paternalism: since Humans err predictably, design defaults that help. Final counsel: organizations, with procedures and vocabulary, can debias better than individuals ever will.
End of the map. Pick the chapters that earn your time.
